title: Value Investing Legend and Warren Buffett Disciple Seth Klarman

segments.text: Speaker 1: Bloomberg Audio Studios, Podcasts, radio news. This is Masters in
Speaker 1: Business with Barry Ritholt on Bloomberg Radio.
Speaker 2: What can I tell you about this week's banger? Seth Klarman,
Speaker 2: legendary value investor out of Boston at the bow Post Group.
Speaker 2: What a fascinating discussion about risk, about the current environment,
Speaker 2: about the Boston Red Sox, about just about anything that
Speaker 2: affects portfolios, distress assets, stocks, bonds, real estate. I thought
Speaker 2: this was fascinating and I know you will also with
Speaker 2: no further ado, my conversation with Seth Klarman. Flarman, Welcome
Speaker 2: to Bloomberg.
Speaker 3: So great to be here.
Speaker 2: Thank you, Berry, Thank you so much. I've been looking
Speaker 2: forward to this for forever. Before we get into your
Speaker 2: investment philosophy and the development of baopost, I have to
Speaker 2: roll back a little bit to your early days economics
Speaker 2: from Cornell, an MBA from Harvard. What was the original
Speaker 2: career plan?
Speaker 4: So I was always drawn to investing, even when I
Speaker 4: was a very young kid. I was interested in the
Speaker 4: baseball statistics. I became aware that there were these other
Speaker 4: columns of numbers in the newspaper and asked my neighbor
Speaker 4: what those were and started to understand and follow the
Speaker 4: stock market a little bit. So, of course I had
Speaker 4: no idea what I was doing, but I was paying
Speaker 4: attention from quite early age. I didn't really ever develop
Speaker 4: a career plan, but I was drawn to the stock market.
Speaker 4: I think I'm drawn to puzzles.
Speaker 3: Berry.
Speaker 4: I like doing word puzzles every day, like solving math puzzles.
Speaker 4: I subscribed still to something called a math puzzle book
Speaker 4: published by Dell. And stock market's a big puzzle. The
Speaker 4: financial markets are a big puzzle. How does it all work?
Speaker 4: And how does the performance of the companies get reflected
Speaker 4: in stock prices? And how can an investor outperform everybody else?
Speaker 4: And so all of that is a piece of what
Speaker 4: drew me in.
Speaker 2: So I'm interested in how you first found that beyond
Speaker 2: the newspaper stock price pages. You grow up in Baltimore,
Speaker 2: your parents divorce when you were relatively young. Mom was
Speaker 2: an English teacher, later a psychiatric social worker. Dad was
Speaker 2: a health economist at John Hopkins and NYU. Was it
Speaker 2: just simply thumbing through the sports pages literally to the
Speaker 2: next set of pages were the stock pages.
Speaker 4: That's literally the numbers on the page attract my attention.
Speaker 4: I would say, you know, I think my origin story
Speaker 4: is a lot like other people in who ended up
Speaker 4: in the investing business, like Warren Buffett, like I think
Speaker 4: Todd Combs, like many others drawn to small businesses wanted
Speaker 4: to make money. So I was delivering newspaper route for
Speaker 4: the Baltimore Sun Papers. I had a snow cone stand
Speaker 4: in my driveway. One summer. I mode lawns, I rake leaves,
Speaker 4: I shoveled snow, I did little carnivals for the neighborhood
Speaker 4: kids whatever. I sold candy at religious school on Tuesdays
Speaker 4: and Thursdays because the kids were starving after school, and
Speaker 4: I would buy it up over the weekend and bring
Speaker 4: it to school and sell it for an arbitrage profit.
Speaker 4: So it was just a pattern of being drawn to
Speaker 4: small business and make money, and over time that led
Speaker 4: to an interest in stock market. About my first stock
Speaker 4: with some barmuts for money when I was around ten years.
Speaker 2: Well, it couldn't have been bar Mitz for money.
Speaker 4: If it wasn't bar Mitz money, then it was a present.
Speaker 4: But then bar Mitz money continued to be.
Speaker 2: So really well then ten years old and.
Speaker 3: I bought a shriff Johnson and Johnson.
Speaker 2: Uh huh, still have it?
Speaker 3: Do not still have it?
Speaker 4: It split three for one, but ultimately I presumably have
Speaker 4: traded that in for something else that I that I
Speaker 4: like better.
Speaker 2: So let's fast forward a little bit to the bow
Speaker 2: Post origin story, which isn't that far ahead. You're only
Speaker 2: twenty five. The urban legends you co found bow Post,
Speaker 2: but reality you were brought in to manage money for
Speaker 2: the four founding families. Still at twenty five, that's a
Speaker 2: kind of shocking thing. Oh, we have all this wealth,
Speaker 2: let's bring in this kid to run our portfolio.
Speaker 3: Right?
Speaker 4: And I would say the same thing if I were,
Speaker 4: if I were in their seats, I would wonder, how
Speaker 4: does this kid know how to do that? So I
Speaker 4: don't think people should generally be starting investment firms at
Speaker 4: age twenty five. And of course I really didn't start
Speaker 4: the firm. The firm was in the process of being created.
Speaker 4: The four clients of the firm that came together. The
Speaker 4: founders had the idea that they would build a firm
Speaker 4: that might go and make investments itself, might hand money
Speaker 4: to others who were in the business already of making investments.
Speaker 4: So I think they wanted to build kind of an
Speaker 4: institutional structure or framework for how to make sure the
Speaker 4: money got managed well given what was then back in
Speaker 4: the early eighties a highly fraught time as you know
Speaker 4: from history, the volatile markets, long history of underperformance of
Speaker 4: the stock market, and real economic uncertainty, stagflation at some
Speaker 4: point and getting worse. Treasury bond yields were getting higher
Speaker 4: and higher. So it was a really fraught moment, and
Speaker 4: I think they wanted to make sure that the money
Speaker 4: they had not only was kept intact, but was accounted
Speaker 4: for clip the coupons and collected ends and all of that.
Speaker 4: The founders were all selling businesses around that time, So
Speaker 4: the serendipity was I was a student at business school.
Speaker 4: Bill Porvu, the PO of Bowpost, was my real estate professor,
Speaker 4: and he and some friends were selling Channel five. He
Speaker 4: was a big investor in that the Metromedia largest sale
Speaker 4: at the time of a TV station to Metromedia. It
Speaker 4: was the ABC affiliate in Boston. A third friend had
Speaker 4: a computer publishing and consulting business. All that was getting sold,
Speaker 4: so they had this pile of twenty seven million dollars
Speaker 4: and the basic job offer I got wasn't come run
Speaker 4: a fund. It was come join us, and let's figure
Speaker 4: out smart things to do with the money.
Speaker 2: So eventually you become the lead partner there. I don't
Speaker 2: know if CEO is the right term.
Speaker 4: I wasn't CEO for the first seven or so years,
Speaker 4: and then it became CEO and effectively got control of
Speaker 4: the firm as sort of a handshake deal where we
Speaker 4: agreed that if I worked hard and did well for
Speaker 4: the clients, that they would recognize that with a stake
Speaker 4: in the business. So I had no stake the day
Speaker 4: it was formed, and ended up with over half.
Speaker 2: Ended up with over half. That's amazing forty something years later.
Speaker 4: And now much less because I'm a big believer in
Speaker 4: sharing the pie with my team.
Speaker 2: Makes a lot of sense. Let's talk a little bit
Speaker 2: about the timing you mentioned. There was a lot of
Speaker 2: turmoil and stagflation the previous sixteen years. I want to
Speaker 2: say the inflation adjusted returns for something like down seventy
Speaker 2: five percent, sixty six to eighty two, something along those lines.
Speaker 2: Eighty two was the beginning of a historic bull market.
Speaker 2: How did that affect how you thought about risk, how
Speaker 2: you thought about opportunities, what were you. What did the
Speaker 2: markets look and feel like in eighty two, when I
Speaker 2: imagine most people were still pretty bearish.
Speaker 4: Yeah, so I think Malcolm Gladwell would look and say,
Speaker 4: nineteen eighty two, what an interesting time to start an
Speaker 4: investment firm. That certainly was a wind at your back
Speaker 4: in terms of being successful the challenges. And you know
Speaker 4: this how it works in the markets is you had
Speaker 4: no idea that you were at the beginning of a
Speaker 4: long bowl market. What you felt was the market hasn't
Speaker 4: done that well, you know, for a long period of time,
Speaker 4: and people were very skeptical about it, and you could
Speaker 4: always point to I think this is probably valuable insight
Speaker 4: is you could always point to things at any moment
Speaker 4: that don't add up, that seem overvalued, that seemed risky,
Speaker 4: and yet we get through most of those things. So
Speaker 4: at the time it didn't feel like a gimme, It
Speaker 4: didn't feel like a lay up hand. But what ended
Speaker 4: up happening was, you know, we tried to make money
Speaker 4: apart from the market. We weren't buying an index. Indexes
Speaker 4: weren't big then anyway, we weren't buying the market. We
Speaker 4: were buying idiosyncratic situations, looking for situation bottom up by
Speaker 4: bottom up miss pricings and that led to us build
Speaker 4: a record. So while it looks just okay compared to
Speaker 4: the market over that period of time, I think we
Speaker 4: would have done okay whether the market had been up
Speaker 4: down our.
Speaker 2: Sideways really interesting. So given you coming off of what
Speaker 2: was an epic bear market and just a whole lot
Speaker 2: of cross current stagflation, super high rates under Vulker, you're
Speaker 2: not that far away in eighty two from the end
Speaker 2: of Vietnam, Watergate, all that malaise. How did that environment
Speaker 2: affect you as a professional investor? How did that change
Speaker 2: how you looked at the world, and what lessons did
Speaker 2: you take from it?
Speaker 4: I would tell you I think every investor needs to
Speaker 4: be a student of history. That it may not repeat exactly,
Speaker 4: but it certainly rhymes and it is very valuable to understand,
Speaker 4: and I think especially financial history for an investor. So
Speaker 4: what are the worst moments? How did we go through
Speaker 4: a market crash in nineteen twenty nine to nineteen thirty
Speaker 4: three and a great depression that lasted close to a decade.
Speaker 4: What must that have been like for the people at
Speaker 4: the time? How would one handle oneself. If you're going
Speaker 4: into a period like that that we know that even
Speaker 4: the greatest acclaim value investor of all time, Benjamin Graham,
Speaker 4: nearly went broke twice during that era. So it's I
Speaker 4: think incumbent on all investors to be thinking and maybe
Speaker 4: holding multiple inconsistent thoughts in their head at the same
Speaker 4: time that I found this interesting opportunity today, this bargain
Speaker 4: price stock for whatever reason, it's out of favor. They
Speaker 4: cut their dividend, it's a spin off, it's a bankrupt
Speaker 4: security that's converting into a new equity. These things tend
Speaker 4: to get mispriced, but you've got a backdrop of from
Speaker 4: time to time, as today, we have a backdrop of
Speaker 4: very expensive market and a bit of you for a
Speaker 4: conditions is that dangerous? It's dangerous, But we're also at
Speaker 4: the cusp of maybe a groundbreaking new technology. So I
Speaker 4: think over the forty years, it's always been some of
Speaker 4: both that you've got a backdrop of something sometimes very depressed,
Speaker 4: sometimes very optimistic, but you've also got individual securities that
Speaker 4: are fluctuating around, maybe creating bottom up opportunity. What I
Speaker 4: deeply believe is that value investors make money staying in
Speaker 4: the bottom up that you might have a top down view,
Speaker 4: you might say, yeah, it could be a bubble, it
Speaker 4: could be a problem. But bottom up is where you're
Speaker 4: going to devote your time. It keeps you anchored that
Speaker 4: if you have a portfolio of bargains, you're probably going to.
Speaker 3: Do okay if you've stress tested.
Speaker 4: Them and if you've been intellectually honest about them and
Speaker 4: they really are bargains.
Speaker 2: So you mentioned Ben Graham, I'm curious as to who
Speaker 2: else were important influences on the development of your investment philosophy.
Speaker 2: I've read about Michael Price and Max Hein, Who affected
Speaker 2: you the most over the years? Who still affects you right?
Speaker 4: So I think reading Ben Graham was certainly a major
Speaker 4: influence on me. I think as he has been on
Speaker 4: essentially everybody in the value investing community. And then Warren Buffett,
Speaker 4: the real life practitioner of Graham as well, And it
Speaker 4: was always heartening to know that somebody like Buffett, who
Speaker 4: seemed to think similarly to how I thought, thought about
Speaker 4: downside risk, thought about the need to stay focused in
Speaker 4: individual companies and not worry so much about the overall market.
Speaker 4: The willingness to hold cash and concurrently like the willingness
Speaker 4: to not have an opinion on everything. I have a
Speaker 4: lot of ideas and I end up with no opinion,
Speaker 4: no position. But once in a while we find something
Speaker 4: that seems way off the beaten path that's really interesting.
Speaker 4: So to watch Warren Buffett do that, I've realized now
Speaker 4: that Warren probably had a certainty of the idea that
Speaker 4: he would compound capital over a long period of time,
Speaker 4: and I think that that is something that Graham probably gave.
Speaker 4: Warren gave me as well, the idea that if you
Speaker 4: protect on the downside, if you don't find yourself getting
Speaker 4: margin calls, frozen in place because you're too exposed, or
Speaker 4: getting massive redemptions because you're down so much, if you
Speaker 4: can position yourself that way, it can leave in a
Speaker 4: position to play offense when even your best competitors might
Speaker 4: be not on the playing field. And that's a huge advantage.
Speaker 4: So I think that Graham a dot is kind of
Speaker 4: a north star. It kind of is a place where
Speaker 4: you can stay focused on what's something worth. You can
Speaker 4: ignore the herd, you can ignore the siren song of
Speaker 4: growth at any price, and of exciting new technologies and
Speaker 4: exciting IPOs and you could ignore all that because you
Speaker 4: have a confidence that I own something that's going to
Speaker 4: be worth more a year or two from now than
Speaker 4: it is today. And that's I think the that's an
Speaker 4: underpinning that lets you follow a value investment strategy.
Speaker 2: So you mentioned downside risk and that we referred to
Speaker 2: before you began in nineteen eighty two. Less than a
Speaker 2: decade later, you publish Margin of Safety nineteen ninety one.
Speaker 2: What led to you, at the ripe old age of
Speaker 2: thirty four to write a book on risk management? What
Speaker 2: was the motivation? How was it initially received, because it's
Speaker 2: become so sought after these days, what was the initial reception? Like?
Speaker 4: Yeah, so I think in retrospect that looks pretty darn presumptuous.
Speaker 4: I got asked to write it by a classmate from
Speaker 4: business school who worked at Harper Collins at the time
Speaker 4: or Harper and Roe, maybe before Harper Collins, and she
Speaker 4: had seen some of my client letters. She said, you know,
Speaker 4: you seem like you'd be a good writer, and you're
Speaker 4: a smart guy. Maybe you'll have something to tell the audience.
Speaker 4: What I really thought was, I'm just updating intelligent investor
Speaker 4: for modern examples and contemporary market. That's decades since that
Speaker 4: book was written, and I thought maybe i'd make it
Speaker 4: a little bit more accessible for the average Joe. I
Speaker 4: don't know whether it accomplished that, but that's what I
Speaker 4: was trying to do. I had no idea if I
Speaker 4: didn't think I would make money from writing the book.
Speaker 4: I mean, as you as an author, you know, we
Speaker 4: get like a buck fifty an hour. But it's a
Speaker 4: great feeling and it's a ton of work, but I
Speaker 4: think ultimately worth it, and you get smarter from the
Speaker 4: act of writing.
Speaker 3: About what you do.
Speaker 4: You can do what you do all day long without
Speaker 4: maybe fully forming the philosophy, but if you want to
Speaker 4: share it with anybody else, it makes you think more
Speaker 4: clearly about what you do.
Speaker 2: Yeah. The former Librarian of Congress, Daniel Borston used to say,
Speaker 2: I write to figure out what I think, And there's
Speaker 2: a lot of truth to that. What was the initial reception, like,
Speaker 2: did people respond or did it kind of land in
Speaker 2: a handful of value? Geeks bought it, but no one else, so.
Speaker 3: It's somewhere in between.
Speaker 4: What happened first was I think my editor got fired
Speaker 4: three different times, so I kept getting new editors. They
Speaker 4: had promised to back the book with advertising, and they didn't,
Speaker 4: so the book landed with a bit of a thud.
Speaker 4: I think it had maybe a very tiny second printing,
Speaker 4: so I think they printed maybe seven thousand copies. I
Speaker 4: ended up buying a bunch of them back from HarperCollins
Speaker 4: by the time they took it off the market and
Speaker 4: the rights somehow reverted back to me.
Speaker 3: What it did do.
Speaker 4: Though, it was bought I think significantly by competitors who
Speaker 4: used it to train their teams. And that was also like,
Speaker 4: is that what I wrote it for? Because I don't mind,
Speaker 4: but it wasn't maybe the starting goal. The starting goal was,
Speaker 4: if you go back to the book, the first half
Speaker 4: of it was about the street and about how they
Speaker 4: treat the average investor and maybe the challenge of whether
Speaker 4: the investor is getting a good deal. And the second
Speaker 4: half is maybe an investment approach, a value oriented approach,
Speaker 4: and how an investor might think about doing that, even
Speaker 4: if they're not a professional investor. So it was successful
Speaker 4: in a weird way. It's sort of because it didn't
Speaker 4: get republished, it developed a bit of a cult following,
Speaker 4: and that's kind of amusing and interesting to me, and
Speaker 4: of course we've reprinted some on our own, so we've
Speaker 4: made it available to our clients and to summer interns
Speaker 4: and to anybody that's connected to the firm.
Speaker 2: So in twenty twenty three, the seventh edition of Security Analysis,
Speaker 2: Ben Graham's Framework for Investing was edited by you and
Speaker 2: in a lot of ways substantially rejiggered. How different is
Speaker 2: this version then Ben Graham's, I mean, obviously the markets changed,
Speaker 2: the economy is so much is different than when he
Speaker 2: was writing. How did you approach this?
Speaker 4: So the earlier edition, the sixth edition, I was co
Speaker 4: editor with Jim Grant and Bruce Greenwald, and the seventh
Speaker 4: edition they asked me to just edit that one. As editor,
Speaker 4: we didn't follow the process that you might follow, because
Speaker 4: we kind of thought of Security Analysis as the Bible
Speaker 4: and we thought we should leave it alone, and what
Speaker 4: we should do is have modern day expert investors right
Speaker 4: commentary about the different chapters, different sections of the book.
Speaker 3: So that's what we did.
Speaker 4: So I think the sixth edition and the seventh both
Speaker 4: have some really great selections by investors. Some of them
Speaker 4: are well known, but some of whom aren't known at all.
Speaker 4: And you know, my former colleague David Abrams is one
Speaker 4: of them. David's contribution in the sixth edition is one
Speaker 4: of the most brilliant things I've ever read, and so
Speaker 4: I felt like we were making we're moving Gramma DoD
Speaker 4: into a different era. The thing that's beautiful about Gramma
Speaker 4: DoD it was written a hundred years ago, give or take,
Speaker 4: and it was written during the depression, and things that
Speaker 4: made sense in a depression haven't made sense every day
Speaker 4: since then because we haven't been a depression most of
Speaker 4: the time since then.
Speaker 3: If at all.
Speaker 4: So it was an update, taking what's valuable why people
Speaker 4: revere the book as a bible, but also making it
Speaker 4: more accessible at more relevant modern day. We expanded it
Speaker 4: to cover some topics that were uncovered. It certainly has
Speaker 4: more international investing, which wasn't really focused on by Graham.
Speaker 4: It talks about some other private investments. It talks about
Speaker 4: some of the changes in financial markets, the latest manias
Speaker 4: and fads and all of that, but also the changes
Speaker 4: in market structure, the changes in asset classes that have
Speaker 4: come into exac distance and all of that. I think
Speaker 4: is a valuable updating of the literature and helps keep
Speaker 4: something relevant that deserves to be relevant while updated because
Speaker 4: in its original Graham and Dodd nineteen thirty four form
Speaker 4: wouldn't be very useful to people.
Speaker 2: Coming up, we continue our conversation with Seth Clarman, CEO
Speaker 2: and portfolio manager at the bow Post Group, discussing the
Speaker 2: firms evolution and philosophy. I'm Barry Ridults. You're listening to
Speaker 2: Masters in Business on Bloomberg Radio. I'm Barry Riddults. You're
Speaker 2: listening to Masters in Business on Bloomberg Radio. Our extra
Speaker 2: special guest this week is Seth Klarman. He's CEO and
Speaker 2: portfolio manager at the bow Post Group, a legendary value
Speaker 2: and distressed investment shop out of Boston, running over twenty
Speaker 2: two billion dollars in assets. So let's talk a little
Speaker 2: bit about the way you think of opportunities and risks.
Speaker 2: During the eight or nine financial crisis, you raised about
Speaker 2: four billion dollars and the research I read you're deploying
Speaker 2: one hundred million dollars a day into distress assets. That
Speaker 2: seems like a big chunk of money. First of all,
Speaker 2: are those numbers remotely accurate? Is that ballpark?
Speaker 3: It's ballpark?
Speaker 4: What I would tell you first of all is we
Speaker 4: had been closed for new clients much of our history,
Speaker 4: but we kept a list in case. And so when
Speaker 4: the market started to fall apart after bear Stearns and
Speaker 4: then after Liman, there were all kinds of things going on,
Speaker 4: and people were in great stress as we entered the
Speaker 4: uncertainty of an economic decline that could have pretty epic proportions.
Speaker 4: As it turned out, it probably was. It certainly was
Speaker 4: the worst decline since the Great Depression, and it stands
Speaker 4: out as kind of the mother of all bear markets
Speaker 4: for anybody in the last hundred years. So the challenge was,
Speaker 4: maybe it's time to take some capital, and the odds
Speaker 4: are increasing every day that we're going to be able
Speaker 4: to deploy it fruitfully.
Speaker 3: So what you said is about right.
Speaker 2: So bear Stearns, if I'm remembering correctly, was spring of
Speaker 2: two thousand and seven. Lehman was September of eight. That's
Speaker 2: not a lot of time from there until March on nine,
Speaker 2: when everything really bottoms. I have three questions about this.
Speaker 2: The first is how quickly were you able to raise capital,
Speaker 2: get the docks signed, and be prepared to deploy that
Speaker 2: as opportunities arose. Doesn't seem like there's a lot of time.
Speaker 4: The team worked heroically and we were able to raise
Speaker 4: about We were able to raise very significant capital within
Speaker 4: a quarter.
Speaker 2: Wow, that's really quickly. Now you mentioned the team, I
Speaker 2: have heard some really interesting rumors and legends. How did
Speaker 2: you put this team together? What was their marching orders?
Speaker 2: How did everybody operate in that period of absolute turmoil
Speaker 2: in Mayhew.
Speaker 4: So we already were an established firm. We've been up
Speaker 4: and running for a couple of decades by then. So
Speaker 4: I had a team in place, and they were deeply knowledgeable, experienced.
Speaker 2: Distressed asset expertise in the group.
Speaker 4: Not everybody on the team has that, but a very
Speaker 4: high percentage of the team has that. And people at
Speaker 4: balpos like being versatile athletes. So we're nimble, we're agile,
Speaker 4: and we cross train just kind of like baseball teams
Speaker 4: are doing now in the minor leagues. They don't want
Speaker 4: you to just be a third basement. They also want
Speaker 4: you to play out field and maybe second if need be,
Speaker 4: And so the same with us that we have people
Speaker 4: that sit in four different groups, as you mentioned, but
Speaker 4: all of them can work on distressed situations. And people
Speaker 4: in the private investments especially love when we're super busy
Speaker 4: in the public markets and we call them in to
Speaker 4: work on a distress credit.
Speaker 2: So big chunk of capital, very aggressively deployed in a
Speaker 2: moment in time when so many people seem to be
Speaker 2: just paralyzed and frozen with fear. Was it just the
Speaker 2: value analytical framework or was it a little broader and
Speaker 2: deeper than that.
Speaker 4: Yeah, I think Barry that the way you're conveying it
Speaker 4: probably comes across as we come in with giant satchels
Speaker 4: of money and handover fists deploy it. It wasn't like
Speaker 4: that at all. It was the same cerebral, methodical, painstaking
Speaker 4: environment that we do every day. So we see things
Speaker 4: trading at lower prices, and we notice that, and we
Speaker 4: look at the fundamentals, and every thing we do at
Speaker 4: Bawtpost is bottom up. Nothing's top down. We're not saying
Speaker 4: probably a good time to be a contray and none
Speaker 4: of that. We're saying, oh, I can buy this bond
Speaker 4: at seventy that I think is covered at par. I
Speaker 4: think people are worried maybe it could have a blip
Speaker 4: or have a problem for a while, but people aren't
Speaker 4: really doubting that there's something there. I think as the
Speaker 4: economy got worse, people may have started to doubt more
Speaker 4: and more. Prices come in more and more, and so
Speaker 4: we're literally able to buy mortgage securities, residential mortgage securities.
Speaker 4: We're able to buy corporate debt, especially of the auto fincos,
Speaker 4: the financial arm of General Motors and Chrysler and Ford,
Speaker 4: and that Lehman goes broke, and that had pieces within
Speaker 4: its capital structure that got very interesting. So we're seeing
Speaker 4: all kinds of things and we're kind of kids in
Speaker 4: a candy store. Sadly, right, it's a tough time. People
Speaker 4: are hurting. But also so as an investor, you're fiduciary
Speaker 4: and you've got to put money to work where you're
Speaker 4: going to benefit your clients. So we're in every case
Speaker 4: stress testing, Hey, if the world got even worse, Hey
Speaker 4: if this turned out to be nineteen thirty three, will
Speaker 4: this investment be okay?
Speaker 3: And that's the.
Speaker 4: Only place where we're where we're making decisions is if
Speaker 4: the downside is protected, and if we can see lots
Speaker 4: of paths to winning, then we're very interested. So we
Speaker 4: found a lot to do in distressed. We also owned equities.
Speaker 4: We also found private investments, and there were just all
Speaker 4: kinds of things worth doing in that era. The challenge
Speaker 4: in investing, I think for everybody is you want to
Speaker 4: make sure that you know those environments are going to
Speaker 4: happen once in a while, and you need to make
Speaker 4: sure you don't blow up during them. And if possible,
Speaker 4: you need to make sure that you'll have capacity to
Speaker 4: buy when the best opportunities become available and maybe your
Speaker 4: competitors are sidelined. And so that's the moment that I
Speaker 4: think investors need to at least have in their heads,
Speaker 4: how are you going to handle that environment? Because if
Speaker 4: you're too exposed, if you're getting margin calls, if you're
Speaker 4: getting massively redeemed because you took the wrong clients and
Speaker 4: they're short term oriented, then you're going to be out
Speaker 4: of commission on that day. So to be around on
Speaker 4: that day and be able to do what we do,
Speaker 4: we just did the same thing we do every day.
Speaker 4: We did a little bigger size.
Speaker 2: So I'm kind of fascinated by the dynamic tension between
Speaker 2: fundamental bottoms up research on a credit or equity by
